cases.passage: The Harappan civilization, which flourished around 2500 BCE, had a well-organized system of governance. Archaeological excavations at Mohenjo-Daro and Harappa have revealed a sophisticated urban planning. The cities were divided into two main sections: the citadel and the lower town. The citadel was the seat of the ruling elite, with large buildings, warehouses, and public baths. The lower town, on the other hand, was the residential area, where artisans, traders, and farmers lived. The Harappans also had a well-developed system of weights and measures, with a standardized system of weights and measures used throughout the civilization. This allowed for easy trade and commerce between different cities and towns.

questions.q: What was the main difference between the citadel and the lower town in Harappan cities?
opts: (A) Citadels had public baths and warehouses, while lower towns had large buildings.
(B) Citadels were residential areas, and lower towns were the seat of the ruling elite.
(C) Citadels were the residential areas, and lower towns were for artisans, traders, and farmers.
(D) Citadels had a well-developed system of weights and measures.
questions.explanation: The citadel was the seat of the ruling elite, and the lower town was the residential area.

questions.q: What was the significance of the standardized system of weights and measures in the Harappan civilization?
opts: (A) It allowed for easy trade and commerce between different cities and towns.
(B) It helped in the development of a well-organized system of governance.
(C) It facilitated the growth of a sophisticated urban planning.
(D) It led to the decline of the Harappan civilization.
questions.explanation: The standardized system of weights and measures allowed for easy trade and commerce between different cities and towns.

questions.q: Which of the following cities was the seat of the ruling elite in the Harappan civilization?
opts: (A) Mohenjo-Daro
(B) Harappa
(C) Both Mohenjo-Daro and Harappa
(D) None of the above
questions.explanation: Both Mohenjo-Daro and Harappa were the seats of the ruling elite in the Harappan civilization.

questions.q: What was the main purpose of the citadel in the Harappan cities?
opts: (A) It was the residential area for artisans, traders, and farmers.
(B) It was the seat of the ruling elite.
(C) It was the commercial center for trade and commerce.
(D) It was the place for public gatherings and celebrations.
questions.explanation: The citadel was the seat of the ruling elite in the Harappan cities.

questions.q: What was the main feature of the lower town in the Harappan cities?
opts: (A) It was the seat of the ruling elite.
(B) It was the commercial center for trade and commerce.
(C) It was the residential area for artisans, traders, and farmers.
(D) It was the place for public gatherings and celebrations.
questions.explanation: The lower town was the residential area for artisans, traders, and farmers in the Harappan cities.

cases.passage: The Indus Valley Civilization was known for its advanced urban planning and architecture. The cities were designed with a sophisticated drainage system, which included public baths, toilets, and drains. The Harappans also built granaries to store grains, which was essential for the survival of the city's population. The granaries were built with a system of ventilation to prevent moisture from accumulating inside. The Indus Valley Civilization also had a well-developed system of trade and commerce, which facilitated the exchange of goods and services between different cities and towns.

questions.q: What was the main feature of the granaries built in the Indus Valley Civilization?
opts: (A) They were built with a system of ventilation to prevent moisture from accumulating inside.
(B) They were built with a sophisticated drainage system.
(C) They were used for storing metals and other precious goods.
(D) They were the seats of the ruling elite.
questions.explanation: The granaries were built with a system of ventilation to prevent moisture from accumulating inside.

questions.q: What was the significance of the public baths in the Indus Valley Civilization?
opts: (A) They were used for ceremonial purposes.
(B) They were used for social gatherings and celebrations.
(C) They were part of the sophisticated drainage system.
(D) They were used for storing grains.
questions.explanation: The public baths were part of the sophisticated drainage system in the Indus Valley Civilization.

questions.q: What was the main purpose of the granaries built in the Indus Valley Civilization?
opts: (A) They were used for storing metals and other precious goods.
(B) They were used for ceremonial purposes.
(C) They were used for storing grains.
(D) They were used for social gatherings and celebrations.
questions.explanation: The granaries were used for storing grains in the Indus Valley Civilization.

questions.q: What was the main feature of the cities in the Indus Valley Civilization?
opts: (A) They were designed with a simple drainage system.
(B) They were designed with a sophisticated drainage system.
(C) They were designed with a well-developed system of trade and commerce.
(D) They were designed with a well-developed system of governance.
questions.explanation: The cities in the Indus Valley Civilization were designed with a sophisticated drainage system.

questions.q: What was the significance of the trade and commerce in the Indus Valley Civilization?
opts: (A) It facilitated the exchange of goods and services between different cities and towns.
(B) It led to the decline of the Indus Valley Civilization.
(C) It was not well-developed in the Indus Valley Civilization.
(D) It was the main feature of the cities in the Indus Valley Civilization.
questions.explanation: The trade and commerce in the Indus Valley Civilization facilitated the exchange of goods and services between different cities and towns.
